content.method: Structured policy-scoping exercise mapping each proposal against explicit problem definitions using stakeholder Delphi elicitation (ministry, opposition, school associations, parent groups) to surface areas of empirical disagreement (scope of impact) versus value disagreement (which problem matters most); track political-consensus durability using comparative case analysis of Polish and prior Hungarian reform reversals.
content.question: What proportion of the reform debate's stated 'problem' (systemic 85% school selectivity, the narrower 9-10% 6/8-year enrollment, age-10 transition disruption, or parental distrust in state upper-primary schools) would each proposal actually address, and is there sufficient political consensus to sustain any chosen reform across multiple electoral cycles?
content.why_it_matters: Different proposals target different definitions of the problem; absent clarity on which problem is being solved, evaluation of 'success' is incoherent and reform risks being reversed by subsequent governments, as occurred in Poland. This is partly an empirical scoping question and partly a value question about which problem framing should govern policy, and the two must be separated.
